text:
Developed by Weaver and Kappelmann in the 1960s at Oak Ridge National Laboratory, the work involved a new process entitled Trivalent Actinide Lanthanide Separation with Phosphorus-reagent Extraction from Aqueous Komplexes (TALSPEAK). At that time the work was undertaken as a less expensive alternative to the TRAMEX process. In TALSPEAK the desired trivalent lanthanides are extracted into an organic phase, while the undesired trivalent actinides are held in the aqueous phase through the use of aminopolycarboxylate complexation. After many years of modification and optimization, the conventional TALSPEAK process based on the use of di-(2-ethylhexyl) phosphoric acid (HDEHP) in 1,4-di-isopropylbenzene and a concentrated lactate solution with diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid (DTPA) has become the standard in aqueous separation processes for the 4f and 5f elements, and can be also applied in the Reverse TALSPEAK process for the actinide selective stripping.

The process achieves a separation factor of about 100 for a single stage between the Am³⁺/Nd³⁺ very difficult pair of elements, and its application has been limited due to pH control within a narrow range of less than two units, as well as a relatively slow mass transfer process that requires high carboxylate buffer concentration (0.5–2 M lactate). Because of the challenges that the TALSPEAK process and the resultant research represents, the past decade has seen a huge effort to characterize and understand in as much detail as possible as many of the chemical interactions that are responsible for the many processes that occur within the aqueous holdback processes that make up TALSPEAK separations. In this paper we highlight these recent studies of the processes involved, and consider how the advances they facilitated will be carried forward in next generation Actinide/Lanthanide separations.
